meta.title: Card
meta.description: A versatile container component presents self-contained information, often combining text, images, and actions, making it ideal for displaying summaries or content previews.

mdx:
## Overview

This versatile container component often integrates text, images, and actions.
Interaction methods differ: some are clickable, others use buttons and links,
and some are purely informational, grouping content without direct interaction.

### Resources

Deep dive into implementation details and access the Nimbus design library.

[Figma library](https://www.figma.com/design/gHbAJGfcrCv7f2bgzUQgHq/NIMBUS-Guidelines?node-id=1793-8790&m=dev)

## Overview

This versatile container component often integrates text, images, and actions.
Interaction methods differ: some are clickable, others use buttons and links,
and some are purely informational, grouping content without direct interaction.

### Resources

a11y.mdx:
## Accessibility

Accessibility ensures that digital content and functionality are usable by
everyone, including people with disabilities, by addressing visual, auditory,
cognitive, and physical limitations.

```jsx live
const App = () => (
  <Card.Root cardPadding="md" borderStyle="outlined" elevation="elevated">
    <Box backgroundColor="#FDE3E3" width="5600" height="500"></Box>
  </Card.Root>
)
```

### Accessibility standards

- **Non-text content:** Provide descriptive alternative text (`alt` attribute)
  for images within cards.
- **Info and relationships:** Use semantic HTML to structure the card content
  logically (e.g., headings, lists).
- **Color contrast:** Ensure sufficient color contrast between text and
  background within the card.
- **Keyboard accessibility:** Ensure the entire card or its primary interactive
  elements are focusable and operable using only a keyboard.
- **Focus visible:** Provide a clear visual focus indicator when the card or its
  interactive elements are focused.
- **Target size:** If the card is interactive, ensure touch target sizes are
  adequate.
- **Meaningful sequence:** Ensure the content within the card is presented in a
  logical order.
- **Labels or instructions:** Provide clear labels or instructions for form
  elements within cards.
- **Semantic HTML:** Use semantic HTML to ensure compatibility with assistive
  technologies.
- **Avoid complex interactive cards:** Avoid making the entire card a single
  interactive element if it contains multiple distinct actions, to ensure proper
  interaction for assistive technology users.

dev.mdx:
## Getting started

### Import

```tsx
import { Box, Card, Link, type CardProps } from '@commercetools/nimbus';
```

### Basic usage

The Card component uses a compound pattern to structure content. You must wrap `Card.Header` and `Card.Content` within `Card.Root`.

```jsx live-dev
const App = () => (
  <Card.Root>
    <Card.Header>Card Title</Card.Header>
    <Card.Content>
      <Text>This is the main card content.</Text>
    </Card.Content>
  </Card.Root>
)
```

## Usage examples

### Size options

The `cardPadding` prop controls the internal spacing of the card. Available sizes are `sm`, `md`, and `lg`.

```jsx live-dev
const App = () => (
  <Stack direction="column" gap="400">
    <Card.Root cardPadding="sm">
      <Card.Header>Small Padding</Card.Header>
      <Card.Content>Compact content spacing.</Card.Content>
    </Card.Root>

    <Card.Root cardPadding="md">
      <Card.Header>Medium Padding</Card.Header>
      <Card.Content>Standard content spacing (default).</Card.Content>
    </Card.Root>

    <Card.Root cardPadding="lg">
      <Card.Header>Large Padding</Card.Header>
      <Card.Content>Spacious content layout.</Card.Content>
    </Card.Root>
  </Stack>
)
```

### Visual variants

Customize the card's appearance using `borderStyle`, `elevation`, and `backgroundStyle`.

```jsx live-dev
const App = () => (
  <Stack direction="column" gap="400">
    <Card.Root borderStyle="outlined" elevation="none">
      <Card.Header>Outlined (Default)</Card.Header>
      <Card.Content>Standard bordered look.</Card.Content>
    </Card.Root>

    <Card.Root borderStyle="none" elevation="elevated">
      <Card.Header>Elevated</Card.Header>
      <Card.Content>Shadow depth without border.</Card.Content>
    </Card.Root>

    <Card.Root backgroundStyle="muted" borderStyle="none">
      <Card.Header>Muted Background</Card.Header>
      <Card.Content>Subtle background color for differentiation.</Card.Content>
    </Card.Root>
  </Stack>
)
```

### Interactive cards

When making a card interactive, avoid placing `onClick` directly on the `Card.Root`. Instead, wrap the card in an anchor tag or use proper ARIA roles to ensure accessibility.

```jsx live-dev
const App = () => (
  <Stack direction="column" gap="400">
    {/* Option 1: Wrapped in a Nimbus Link (Recommended for navigation) */}
    <Link 
      href="#" 
      textDecoration="none" 
      color="inherit" 
      display="block"
      _hover={{ textDecoration: 'none' }}
      aria-label="Navigate to details"
    >
      <Card.Root borderStyle="outlined" elevation="elevated">
        <Card.Header>Navigational Card</Card.Header>
        <Card.Content>
          This entire card acts as a link using the system Link component.
        </Card.Content>
      </Card.Root>
    </Link>

    {/* Option 2: Interactive element with ARIA (For actions) */}
    <Box
      role="button"
      tabIndex={0}
      onClick={() => alert("Card clicked")}
      onKeyDown={(e) => {
        if (e.key === "Enter" || e.key === " ") {
          e.preventDefault();
          alert("Card clicked");
        }
      }}
      cursor="pointer"
      aria-label="Trigger action"
    >
      <Card.Root borderStyle="outlined">
        <Card.Header>Action Card</Card.Header>
        <Card.Content>
          Behaves like a button with keyboard support (Tab, Enter, Space).
        </Card.Content>
      </Card.Root>
    </Box>
  </Stack>
)
```

## Component requirements

## Accessibility

The `Card` component handles most accessibility requirements internally, rendering semantic `div` elements by default. It acts as a generic container.

- **Headings**: Ensure the content within `Card.Header` follows your page's heading hierarchy (e.g., use `Heading` component or `h3`, `h4` tags).
- **Interactive Cards**: If a card is interactive (clickable), do not attach `onClick` directly to the card div if possible. Instead, wrap the card in a link or button, or ensure proper ARIA roles (`role="button"`) and keyboard handling (`tabIndex={0}`, Enter/Space key listeners) are implemented.

If your use case requires tracking and analytics for this component, it is good practice to add a **persistent**, **unique** id to the component:

```tsx
const PERSISTENT_ID = "project-summary-card";

export const Example = () => (
  <Card.Root id={PERSISTENT_ID}>
    <Card.Header>Summary</Card.Header>
    <Card.Content>...</Card.Content>
  </Card.Root>
);
```

#### Keyboard navigation

Standard Cards are not focusable. Interactive content inside the card (buttons, links) follows the default focus order.

## API reference

<PropsTable id="Card" />

## Testing your implementation

These examples demonstrate how to test your implementation when using Card within your application. As the component's internal functionality is already tested by Nimbus, these patterns help you verify your integration and application-specific logic.

### Basic Rendering Tests

Verify the card displays the correct title and content

```tsx
import { describe, it, expect } from "vitest";
import { render, screen } from "@testing-library/react";
import { Card, NimbusProvider } from "@commercetools/nimbus";

describe("Card - Basic rendering", () => {
  it("renders project details", () => {
    render(
      <NimbusProvider>
        <Card.Root>
          <Card.Header>Project X</Card.Header>
          <Card.Content>Status: Active</Card.Content>
        </Card.Root>
      </NimbusProvider>
    );

    expect(screen.getByText("Project X")).toBeInTheDocument();
    expect(screen.getByText("Status: Active")).toBeInTheDocument();
  });

  it("renders with header only", () => {
    render(
      <NimbusProvider>
        <Card.Root>
          <Card.Header>Card Title</Card.Header>
        </Card.Root>
      </NimbusProvider>
    );

    expect(screen.getByText("Card Title")).toBeInTheDocument();
  });

  it("renders with content only", () => {
    render(
      <NimbusProvider>
        <Card.Root>
          <Card.Content>This is the main content.</Card.Content>
        </Card.Root>
      </NimbusProvider>
    );

    expect(screen.getByText("This is the main content.")).toBeInTheDocument();
  });
});
```


## Resources

- [Storybook](https://nimbus-storybook.vercel.app/?path=/docs/components-card--docs)

guidelines.mdx: ## Guidelines Card component guidelines focus on providing a clear, concise, and visually distinct container for related content, ensuring scannability, clear actions, and adaptability across various layouts and screen sizes. ### Best practices - **Clear purpose:** Each card should represent a single, distinct piece of content or concept. Avoid combining multiple unrelated ideas into one card. - **Concise content:** Keep the information within a card brief and scannable. Use clear headings, short descriptions, and only essential details. - **Visual hierarchy:** Establish a clear visual hierarchy to guide the user's eye. Use appropriate typography, spacing, and visual cues to prioritize important information. - **Actionable elements:** If the card requires user interaction, clearly present the primary action(s) with prominent buttons or links. Limit the number of actions to avoid overwhelming the user. - **Consistent styling:** Maintain consistent styling for cards. This includes borders, shadows, typography, and spacing. - **Responsive design:** Ensure cards adapt well to different screen sizes and orientations. Consider how the layout will change on mobile devices. ### Usage The strength of using cards in a UI lies in their ability to present information in concise, digestible chunks, making it easy for users to quickly scan, understand, and interact with related content, especially in responsive layouts. > [!TIP]\ > When to use - **Displaying a collection of related items:** When you have multiple, distinct items that share a similar structure or theme (e.g., product listings, documentation, user profiles). - **Presenting scannable summaries:** When you need to provide a quick overview of information, allowing users to grasp the key details at a glance. - **Highlighting key information:** When you want to draw attention to important pieces of content or features. - **Facilitating task completion:** When a card can contain actions that allow users to quickly interact with or manage the displayed information. - **Creating a visually organized layout:** When you need to break down complex information into digestible chunks and create a structured, modular design. - **Responsive design:** When you need a component that adapts well to various screen sizes and can be easily rearranged on different devices. - **Content discovery:** When you want to encourage users to explore different pieces of content. > [!CAUTION]\ > When not to use - **Presenting linear processes:** When users need to follow a specific, sequential flow (e.g., a step-by-step form or a tutorial). Other patterns like steppers are more appropriate. - **Displaying single, detailed items:** When you need to present a lot of in-depth information about one specific item. A dedicated page is usually better. - **Primary navigation:** Using cards for main site navigation can be overwhelming and make it difficult for users to understand the site's structure. - **Simple lists:** For basic lists of items with minimal information, a simple list or table might be more efficient and less visually heavy. - **When content is highly interrelated and needs comparison:** If users need to compare specific attributes across multiple items, a table might be a better choice. - **Overcrowding the UI with too many cards:** Using too many cards on a page can lead to visual clutter and make it hard for users to focus on what's important. - **Forcing content into a card structure:** If the content doesn't naturally fit into a card format, trying to force it can lead to awkward design and a poor user experience. ### Content Content within cards should be concise and focused, prioritizing key information and clear actions to ensure scannability and quick user comprehension. Consider the card's purpose and limit content to only the most relevant details, using clear headings and short descriptions. > [!TIP]\ > **Do** > > - Cards are best for presenting self-contained pieces of information. > - Design cards with clear headings, concise text, and a logical visual > hierarchy for easy scanning. ```jsx live const App = () => ( Discount templates Templates allow you to get started quickly and easily by displaying only what is needed for creating your discount. {/* Options would go here */} ); ``` > [!CAUTION]\ > **Don't** > > - Avoid cramming too much information into a single card. Each card should > focus on a single subject. > - Long, scrolling cards can be cumbersome; prioritize key information. > - Don't use a card to compare information. ```jsx live const App = () => ( Discount templates Templates allow you to get started quickly and easily by displaying only what is needed for creating your discount. {/* Options would go here */} Product discount A product discount applies a percentage-off (relative) or amount-ff (absolute) discount to a specific product or subset of products before adding them to the cart. They are useful for displaying discounted or sale prices on a product info page ); ``` ### Actions Actions within a card should be limited and focused, prioritizing the primary action related to the card's content to avoid overwhelming the user. Ensure actions are clearly labeled and visually prominent to facilitate quick interaction. > [!TIP]\ > **Do** > > - If interaction is needed, provide obvious and limited actions. > - Users often assume cards are clickable, especially when they contain visual > cues that suggest interactivity, like buttons, links, or hover effects. > However, this assumption isn't universal and can vary depending on the > context and user experience. ```jsx live const App = () => ( Learn more about products Discover what a product list is and how to manage it. ); ``` > [!CAUTION]\ > **Don't** > > - Don't use too many actions for the user within cards. > - Cards are generally not suitable for primary site navigation. ```jsx live const App = () => ( {/* Main Card */} See your products You have modified products that are waiting to be published {/* Buttons */} {/* Bottom Row Cards */} {/* Products Card */} {/* Half width approx */} Products {/* Discounts Card */} {/* Half width approx */} Discounts ); ```
